summary:
The bow and arrow represents one of the most consequential technological developments in prehistoric human history, and Africa preserves some of the strongest evidence for its earliest use. Backed microliths — the small, geometrically shaped stone inserts hafted as arrowheads — appear in southern African assemblages dating back tens of thousands of years, though unambiguous composite projectile technology consistent with the bow became more widespread and better documented in African contexts during the terminal Pleistocene and early Holocene, spanning roughly 12,000 to 8,000 BCE. Sites such as those associated with the later phases of the Capsian and related North African industries, as well as the microlithic tool traditions of sub-Saharan Africa, show the proliferation of the small backed bladelets that served as lethal projectile tips.

The bow and arrow conferred significant advantages to hunter-gatherer communities in Africa. Unlike thrusting spears or even atlatl-propelled darts, bows allowed hunters to bring down game at greater distances with reduced risk of injury and with less expenditure of energy in the pursuit phase. The technology was particularly well suited to the varied African environments of the early Holocene — from the grassy margins of the greening Sahara to woodland savannas — where animals could be hunted from concealment. The spread of microlithic technology across Africa during this period likely reflects, in part, the growing importance of composite projectile weapons as a primary subsistence strategy for mobile foraging communities adapting to post-glacial ecological change.
